title: Mahadhavala

title: Kashayapahuda

title: Tattvartha Sutra
author: Acharya Umasvati
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the dravyanuyog tradition.
sample_passage: Right faith, right knowledge, and right conduct together constitute the path to liberation (samyag-darshana-jnana-charitrani mokshamargah). The substances (dravya) are six: soul, matter, medium of motion, medium of rest, space, and time. The influx (asrava), bondage (bandha), stoppage (samvara), shedding (nirjara) and liberation (moksha) of karma define the spiritual journey.

title: Sarvarthasiddhi
author: Acharya Pujyapada
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the dravyanuyog tradition.

title: Tattvartha Rajavartika
author: Acharya Akalankadeva
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the dravyanuyog tradition.

title: Tattvartha Shlokavartika
author: Acharya Vidyananda
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the dravyanuyog tradition.
sample_passage: Reality is many-sided (anekanta); every substance possesses infinite attributes and modes. Through the doctrine of conditional predication (syadvada) and the standpoints (naya), one apprehends truth without one-sided dogmatism. Valid knowledge (pramana) grasps the whole; a naya grasps a part.
sample_passage_translated: वस्तु अनेकान्तात्मक है; स्याद्वाद से ही उसका यथार्थ ज्ञान होता है।

title: Gommatasara (Jiva-kanda)
author: Acharya Nemichandra
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the dravyanuyog tradition.

title: Gommatasara (Karma-kanda)
author: Acharya Nemichandra
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the dravyanuyog tradition.

title: Samayasara
author: Acharya Kundakunda
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the dravyanuyog tradition.
sample_passage: The soul (jiva) is, by its very nature, pure consciousness and is forever distinct from karmic matter. Though bound in worldly existence, the soul never truly becomes the karma; it only appears so from the conventional standpoint (vyavahara naya). From the absolute standpoint (nishchaya naya), the soul is the knower and seer, untouched by the modifications of matter.
sample_passage_translated: जीव अपने स्वभाव से शुद्ध चैतन्यस्वरूप है और कर्म-पुद्गल से सर्वथा भिन्न है।

title: Pravachanasara
author: Acharya Kundakunda
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the dravyanuyog tradition.
sample_passage: He who knows the soul as it truly is — pure, conscious, and self-luminous — attains equanimity. Knowledge and the knower are not separate; the soul is itself knowledge. By abandoning attachment and aversion (raga-dvesha) and resting in pure cognition, the practitioner moves toward liberation.
sample_passage_translated: जो आत्मा को शुद्ध चैतन्यरूप जानता है वही समता को प्राप्त करता है।

title: Niyamasara
author: Acharya Kundakunda
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the dravyanuyog tradition.

title: Panchastikaya-sara
author: Acharya Kundakunda
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the dravyanuyog tradition.

title: Ashtapahud
author: Acharya Kundakunda
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the dravyanuyog tradition.

title: Rayansara
author: Acharya Kundakunda
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the charananuyog tradition.

title: Dash Bhakti
author: Acharya Kundakunda
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the charananuyog tradition.

title: Baras-anuvekkha
author: Acharya Kundakunda
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the charananuyog tradition.
sample_passage: Contemplate the twelve reflections (anuprekshas): the impermanence of all things (anitya), the helplessness of the soul (asharana), the cycle of rebirth (samsara), aloneness (ekatva), separateness (anyatva), the impurity of the body (ashuchi), and so on. Such contemplation loosens attachment and steadies the mind toward dispassion.
sample_passage_translated: बारह भावनाओं का चिंतन वैराग्य को दृढ़ करता है।

title: Aptamimansa
author: Acharya Samantabhadra
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the dravyanuyog tradition.

title: Nyayavinischaya
author: Acharya Akalankadeva
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the dravyanuyog tradition.

title: Siddhivinischaya
author: Acharya Akalankadeva
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the dravyanuyog tradition.

title: Praman Sangrah
author: Acharya Akalankadeva
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the dravyanuyog tradition.

title: Ashtashati
author: Acharya Akalankadeva
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the dravyanuyog tradition.

title: Ashtasahastri
author: Acharya Vidyananda
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the dravyanuyog tradition.

title: Dravyasamgraha
author: Acharya Nemichandra
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the dravyanuyog tradition.
sample_passage: There are six substances (dravya): jiva (soul), pudgala (matter), dharma (medium of motion), adharma (medium of rest), akasha (space) and kala (time). The soul is characterized by consciousness (chetana) and is the only substance possessing upayoga — the active functions of knowing and perceiving.
sample_passage_translated: द्रव्य छह हैं: जीव, पुद्गल, धर्म, अधर्म, आकाश और काल।

title: Paramatma Prakash

description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the dravyanuyog tradition.

title: Harivamsa Purana

title: Mahapurana

title: Pandava Purana

title: Parshvanatha Purana

title: Trishashti-Shalakapurusha Charitra
author: Acharya Hemachandra
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the prathamanuyoga tradition.

title: Kalpa-sutra
author: Acharya Bhadrabahu
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the prathamanuyoga tradition.

title: Ratnakaranda Shravakachara
author: Acharya Samantabhadra
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the charananuyog tradition.
sample_passage: The conduct of a householder (shravaka) rests upon right faith and the twelve vows: five minor vows (anuvrata) of non-violence, truth, non-stealing, chastity and non-possession, together with the three guna-vratas and four shiksha-vratas. Non-violence (ahimsa) is the foremost, the root of all virtue.
sample_passage_translated: श्रावक का आचरण सम्यग्दर्शन और बारह व्रतों पर आधारित है; अहिंसा सर्वोपरि है।

title: Purushartha-siddhyupaya
author: Acharya Amritachandra
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the charananuyog tradition.
sample_passage: Non-violence is the supreme religion (ahimsa paramo dharmah). To injure any living being through carelessness (pramada) is himsa; to act with vigilance and compassion is ahimsa. True non-violence begins in the mind, before any outward act, for it is the intention that binds or frees the soul.
sample_passage_translated: प्रमादयोग से प्राणियों का घात ही हिंसा है; अप्रमत्त भाव ही अहिंसा है।

title: Ishtopadesh
author: Acharya Pujyapada
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the charananuyog tradition.
sample_passage: Sorrow is born not from things themselves but from our clinging (raga) to what is other than the self. The one who looks outward for peace is like a person searching for a lamp in darkness while holding one in hand. Turn inward: the self is its own refuge. What is gained is lost, what is met is parted — knowing this, the wise neither grasp in gain nor break in loss.
sample_passage_translated: दुख वस्तुओं से नहीं, पर-पदार्थों के राग से उत्पन्न होता है।

title: Samadhi Tantra
author: Acharya Pujyapada
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the charananuyog tradition.
sample_passage: You are not this body, nor these passing moods of anger, fear, or sorrow; you are the conscious knower who witnesses them all. When the mind is agitated, the wise one steps back and observes the disturbance as a cloud passing across an unchanging sky. Resting in this witnessing self (the knower), equanimity (samata) arises, and the grip of grief and craving loosens of its own accord.
sample_passage_translated: तू यह शरीर नहीं, न ये क्षणिक भाव; तू तो इन सबका साक्षी चैतन्य है।

title: Atmanushasan
author: Acharya Gunabhadra
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the charananuyog tradition.
sample_passage: Anger (krodha) scorches first the one who holds it, before it ever touches another — as fire burns the vessel that carries it. Pride (maana) blinds, deceit (maya) entangles, and greed (lobha) is a thirst that drinking only deepens. These four passions (kasaya) are not enemies to be hated but habits to be gently unlearned through self-awareness and forgiveness (kshama). Govern the self with patience, not with violence toward the self.
sample_passage_translated: क्रोध पहले उसी को जलाता है जो उसे धारण करता है।

title: Moksha Marg Prakashak
author: Pandit Todarmal
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the charananuyog tradition.

title: Mulachara
author: Acharya Vattakera
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the charananuyog tradition.

title: Triloksara
author: Acharya Nemichandra
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the karnanuyoga tradition.

title: Jambudveepa Pragnapti
author: Canonical
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the karnanuyoga tradition.
sample_passage: At the center of the middle world lies Jambudvipa, a continent ringed by oceans, with the golden Mount Meru at its heart. The cosmos (loka) is uncreated and eternal, structured into the lower, middle and upper realms, each described in precise measure across vast cycles of time (kalpa).
sample_passage_translated: मध्यलोक के केंद्र में जम्बूद्वीप है, जिसके मध्य में सुमेरु पर्वत स्थित है।

title: Svayambhu Stotra
author: Acharya Samantabhadra
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the karnanuyoga tradition.

title: Bhaktamara Stotra
author: Acharya Manatunga
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the karnanuyoga tradition.
sample_passage: Having bowed at the feet of the first Tirthankara, whose radiance dispels the darkness of delusion, the devotee praises the Lord who is the refuge of all beings. As the morning sun reveals the lotus, so devotion to the Jina awakens the soul's innate purity.

title: Laghu Nayachakra
author: Acharya Devsena
description: A foundational Digambar Jain text in the karnanuyoga tradition.
